turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Jutice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 21-1164 Wilkins v. United States.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Congress creates procedural rules to promote the orderly course of litigation. Such claim processing rules require parties to take certain steps and raise arguments at certain times. In contrast, jurisdictional rules depart from these ordinary principles of adversarial litigation. Such rules can be raised at any time even by parties that disclaim them or on appeal causing inefficiency, waste and unfairness. Accordingly unless ordinary tools of statutory interpretation find a clear statement that Congress made procedural rules jurisdictional, the provision is a non-jurisdictional claim processing rule. This case involves the Quiet Title Act's 12 year time limit to file claims challenging the government's rights in real property section 2409a(g). Most time limits are non-jurisdictional and this one is no exception. The provision contains mundane language about when an action must be commenced and the provision is not located in a section of the statute addressing jurisdiction nor does it reference any jurisdictional provision. The government barely disputes this. Instead the government claims that prior decisions of this Court already established that Section 2409a(g) is jurisdictional. It is true that this Court will not revisit a definitive earlier interpretation about jurisdiction without due regard for principles of stare decisis. Yet the prior decision must be just that definitive. In the past, this Court has loosely used the term jurisdiction when it's simply meant that some threshold factor was not met. This Court does not afford precedential way to such drive by jurisdictional rulings. Instead, the Court asked if a prior decision addressed whether a provision was technically jurisdictional and whether anything turned on that result. None of the cases the government offers fits this profile. Block v. North Dakota was about whether states were subject to the Act's time limit not about whether the limit was subject matter jurisdiction. United States v. Mottaz addressed only whether a claim arose under the Quiet Title Act or other statute and whether this plaintiff had brought suit within the requisite 12 years. Finally United States v. Beggerly address whether equitable tolling was available. A question the Court could have avoided entirely if prior cases definitively establish that Section 2409a(g) was jurisdictional. For these and other reasons set forth in the opinion, the Court holds that Section 2409a(g) is a non-jurisdictional claim processing rule and the Court vacates and remands for further consideration of this case. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion in which the Chief Justice and Justice Alito have joined.
